ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a choy sum entry as a raw vegetable; the prepared stir-fried dish is matched to the closest parent raw ingredient node. No exact prepared-dish code exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific choy sum stir-fry code; A00LC (cabbages and related vegetables, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for choy sum or this specific stir-fried vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried choy sum is approximately 200 g, representing a shared side dish for one to two persons in a Chinese meal setting.

nutrient_source.title: Choy Sum, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis (cooked, boiled) adapted for stir-fry preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists cooked choy sum (cai xin) per 100 g. Values for the stir-fried restaurant version are adapted from HPB cooked choy sum data with a modest upward adjustment to sodium and fat to reflect oyster sauce and oil used in restaurant stir-frying, consistent with USDA FoodData Central NDB 11116 (Chinese broccoli, cooked) as a secondary cross-reference. Energy and macros are primarily anchored to HPB Foodo cooked choy sum.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for cooked leafy brassica vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried choy sum. The University of Sydney GI Database consistently reports GI values of 10–20 for cooked non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables (e.g., bok choy, Chinese broccoli). A GI of 15 is used as a defensible analogous estimate for stir-fried choy sum. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 3.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.14, rounded to 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Stir-fried choy sum prepared with oyster sauce contributes approximately 560 mg sodium per 200 g restaurant serving; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce or choose steamed preparations to limit sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, stir-fried choy sum has a negligible glycemic load and is an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High folate, vitamin C, and low saturated fat with beneficial phytonutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Choy sum provides folate, vitamin C, and vitamin A with minimal saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; the primary concern is sodium from restaurant-style preparation, which should be moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Emerald Tender Greens (翠玉菜心, Cui Yu Cai Xin) is a popular Chinese vegetable dish served in Singaporean Chinese restaurants, featuring choy sum (cai xin, Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis) stir-fried in a wok over high heat with garlic, vegetable oil, and oyster sauce. The dish is prized for its vibrant emerald-green colour, tender yet slightly crisp stems, and mildly sweet, fresh flavour with a savoury umami finish from the oyster sauce. Choy sum is one of the most commonly consumed leafy green vegetables in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable providing approximately 52 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful amounts of vitamin C (28 mg), vitamin A (95 mcg RAE), folate (55 mcg DFE), calcium (90 mg), and iron (1.2 mg). The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary nutritional concern in restaurant preparations is sodium from oyster sauce and salt, which can reach 280 mg per 100 g. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat, supporting cardiovascular health. It is commonly served as a shared side dish in a Chinese family-style meal alongside rice and protein dishes. The name 翠玉菜心 (Cui Yu Cai Xin) translates literally as 'jade-green vegetable heart', reflecting the tender inner stems of the choy sum plant that are harvested before flowering.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for the specific restaurant dish 'Emerald Tender Greens' was found; values are adapted from HPB cooked choy sum data with adjustments for restaurant stir-fry preparation (added oil and oyster sauce). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked leafy brassica vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo cooked choy sum data, adjusted upward for sodium (oyster sauce) and fat (cooking oil) typical of restaurant stir-fry preparation. USDA FoodData Central NDB 11116 (Chinese broccoli, cooked) used as secondary cross-reference. 2. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables; no direct measurement for stir-fried choy sum exists in the approved GI database. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12 by nature; naturally zero). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — choy sum contains no meaningful vitamin D; naturally zero). 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used; naturally zero). 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food; naturally zero). 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from vegetable oil composition). 8. The dish name 翠玉菜心 is preserved from the input record as the Chinese name. 9. Allergen note: oyster sauce contains molluscs and may contain gluten (wheat); is_gluten_free set to false accordingly. 10. Halal status: generally considered halal as no pork products are used, though oyster sauce brand may vary; marked halal with caveat.
